Prefixes and Suffixes

The Language of Anatomy & Physiology

1777566980

Introduction

Medical terminology is built from prefixes, root words, and suffixes. Understanding these word parts is super helpful because it allows students and healthcare workers to understand unfamiliar terms quickly without memorizing every word separately. By learning common prefixes and suffixes, we can identify body locations, conditions, diseases, treatments, physiological functions, and chemical processes.

Why This Is Helpful

Learning prefixes and suffixes helps people:

  • Understand anatomy vocabulary faster.
  • Break down difficult medical terms.
  • Improve communication in healthcare settings.
  • Recognize diseases, procedures, body parts, physiology terms, and chemistry-related processes.
  • Understand scientific vocabulary used in anatomy, physiology, and biochemistry.
  • Build confidence when studying anatomy and physiology.

For example:

  • Adrenal = ad- (near) + renal (kidney)
  • Hypoglycemia = hypo- (low) + glyc (sugar)
  • Epidermis = epi- (outer) + dermis (skin)

Common Prefixes (Page 1)

Examples:

  • Pericardium = around the heart
  • Subcutaneous = under the skin
  • Intercostal = between the ribs
Prefix Meaning Example
ad- near, toward adrenal
hypo- low, below normal hypoglycemia
hyper- high, excessive hypertension
epi- upon, outer epidermis
endo- inside, within endocrine
peri- around pericardium
sub- under subcutaneous
inter- between intercostal
intra- within intravenous
brady- slow bradycardia

Common Prefixes (Page 2)

Examples:

  • Tachypnea = fast breathing
  • Microorganism = very small organism
  • Bilateral = both sides
Prefix Meaning Example
tachy- fast tachycardia
dys- abnormal, painful dysplasia
neo- new neonatal
poly- many polyuria
oligo- few, little oliguria
uni- one unilateral
bi- two bilateral
tri- three triceps
micro- small microscope
macro- large macrocyte

Common Suffixes (Page 3)

Examples:

  • Dermatitis = inflammation of the skin
  • Neuralgia = nerve pain
  • Cardiomegaly = enlarged heart
  • Hemolysis = breakdown of red blood cells
  • Amylase = enzyme that digests starch

Many suffixes are also used in physiology and chemistry. For example, -lysis means breakdown or separation, such as in electrolysis or hemolysis.

Suffix Meaning Example
-itis inflammation arthritis
-ology study of cardiology
-ectomy surgical removal appendectomy
-algia pain neuralgia
-emia blood condition anemia
-osis abnormal condition cyanosis
-pathy disease neuropathy
-megaly enlargement cardiomegaly
-scopy visual examination endoscopy
-gram record/image electrocardiogram
-lysis breakdown, separation hemolysis
-ase enzyme lactase
-physis growth, natural process epiphysis

Conclusion

Prefixes and suffixes are essential tools for understanding anatomy, physiology, chemistry, and medical language. They make learning easier, save time, and help students understand hundreds of terms by knowing just a few word parts. Terms such as -lysis (breakdown), -ase (enzyme), and -physis (growth) show how these word parts are used beyond anatomy. Mastering them is valuable for anyone studying healthcare or science.

References

  1. OpenStax. Anatomy & Physiology.
  2. Ehrlich A, Schroeder CL. Medical Terminology for Health Professions.
  3. Leonard PC. Introduction to Medical Terminology.
  4. Taber’s Cyclopedic Medical Dictionary.

https://quarto.org
